question: 4. The flag of the United Nations consists of a polar map of the world, with the North Pole as center, extending approximately to \( 45^{\circ} \) South Latitude. The parallels of latitude are concentric circles with radii proportional to their co-latitudes. Australia is near the periphery of the map and is intersected by the parallel of latitude \( 30^{\circ} \mathrm{S} \). In the very close vicinity of this parallel how much are East and West distances exaggerated as compared to North and South distances?
solution:
Solution. Let a point on the earth with co-latitude \( \alpha \) and longitude \( \theta \) be represented by a point on the flag map with polar coordinates \( k \alpha \) and \( \theta \) where \( k \) is a constant of proportionality.
Let \( R \) be the radius of the earth. A meridian on the earth has length \( \pi R \) and on the (complete) flag map is represented by a segment of length \( \pi k \). Hence a short distance \( d \) in the North-South direction is represented on the flag map by the distance \( d_{1}=k d / R \).
On the earth the small circle of latitude \( 30^{\circ} S(= \) co-latitude \( 2 \pi / 3 \) ) is represented on the flag map by a circle of radius \( 2 \pi k / 3 \) and of circumference \( 4 \pi^{2} k / 3 \). On the earth that circle has length \( 2 \pi R \sin (2 \pi / 3)= \) \( \pi R \sqrt{3} \).

Hence a short distance \( d \) in the East-West direction is represented on the flag map by a distance
\[
d \frac{\frac{4 \pi^{2} k}{3}}{\pi R \sqrt{3}}=\frac{4 \pi k d}{3 \sqrt{3} R}=\frac{4 \pi}{3 \sqrt{3}} d_{1} .
\]

Therefore distances in the East-West direction at latitude \( 30^{\circ} \) S are magnified relative to distances in the North-South direction by the factor \( 4 \pi / 3 \sqrt{3} \sim 2.42 \).

descriptive_long.question: 4. The flag of the United Nations consists of a polar map of the world, with the North Pole as center, extending approximately to \( 45^{\circ} \) South Latitude. The parallels of latitude are concentric circles with radii proportional to their co-latitudes. Australia is near the periphery of the map and is intersected by the parallel of latitude \( 30^{\circ} \mathrm{S} \). In the very close vicinity of this parallel how much are East and West distances exaggerated as compared to North and South distances?
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let a point on the earth with co-latitude \( anglecolat \) and longitude \( anglelong \) be represented by a point on the flag map with polar coordinates \( scalefactor \, anglecolat \) and \( anglelong \) where \( scalefactor \) is a constant of proportionality.
Let \( earthradius \) be the radius of the earth. A meridian on the earth has length \( \pi \, earthradius \) and on the (complete) flag map is represented by a segment of length \( \pi \, scalefactor \). Hence a short distance \( smallstep \) in the North-South direction is represented on the flag map by the distance \( mappedstep = scalefactor \, smallstep / earthradius \).
On the earth the small circle of latitude \( 30^{\circ} S (= \) co-latitude \( 2 \pi / 3 ) \) is represented on the flag map by a circle of radius \( 2 \pi \, scalefactor / 3 \) and of circumference \( 4 \pi^{2} \, scalefactor / 3 \). On the earth that circle has length \( 2 \pi \, earthradius \sin(2 \pi / 3) = \pi \, earthradius \sqrt{3} \).

Hence a short distance \( smallstep \) in the East-West direction is represented on the flag map by a distance
\[
smallstep \frac{\frac{4 \pi^{2} \, scalefactor}{3}}{\pi \, earthradius \sqrt{3}} = \frac{4 \pi \, scalefactor \, smallstep}{3 \sqrt{3} \, earthradius} = \frac{4 \pi}{3 \sqrt{3}} \, mappedstep .
\]

Therefore distances in the East-West direction at latitude \( 30^{\circ} \) S are magnified relative to distances in the North-South direction by the factor \( 4 \pi / (3 \sqrt{3}) \approx 2.42 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 4. The flag of the United Nations consists of a polar map of the world, with the North Pole as center, extending approximately to \( 45^{\circ} \) South Latitude. The parallels of latitude are concentric circles with radii proportional to their co-latitudes. Australia is near the periphery of the map and is intersected by the parallel of latitude \( 30^{\circ} \mathrm{S} \). In the very close vicinity of this parallel how much are East and West distances exaggerated as compared to North and South distances?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let a point on the earth with co-latitude \( sunflower \) and longitude \( backpack \) be represented by a point on the flag map with polar coordinates \( shoelaces \, sunflower \) and \( backpack \) where \( shoelaces \) is a constant of proportionality.
Let \( paintbrush \) be the radius of the earth. A meridian on the earth has length \( \pi paintbrush \) and on the (complete) flag map is represented by a segment of length \( \pi shoelaces \). Hence a short distance \( windstorm \) in the North-South direction is represented on the flag map by the distance \( watermelon=shoelaces \, windstorm / paintbrush \).
On the earth the small circle of latitude \( 30^{\circ} S(= \) co-latitude \( 2 \pi / 3 \) ) is represented on the flag map by a circle of radius \( 2 \pi shoelaces / 3 \) and of circumference \( 4 \pi^{2} shoelaces / 3 \). On the earth that circle has length \( 2 \pi paintbrush \sin (2 \pi / 3)= \) \( \pi paintbrush \sqrt{3} \).

Hence a short distance \( windstorm \) in the East-West direction is represented on the flag map by a distance
\[
windstorm \frac{\frac{4 \pi^{2} shoelaces}{3}}{\pi paintbrush \sqrt{3}}=\frac{4 \pi shoelaces \, windstorm}{3 \sqrt{3} paintbrush}=\frac{4 \pi}{3 \sqrt{3}} watermelon .
\]

Therefore distances in the East-West direction at latitude \( 30^{\circ} \) S are magnified relative to distances in the North-South direction by the factor \( 4 \pi / 3 \sqrt{3} \sim 2.42 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Problem: <<<\n4. The flag of the United Nations consists of a polar map of the world, with the North Pole as center, extending approximately to \( 45^{\\circ} \) South Latitude. The parallels of latitude are concentric circles with radii proportional to their co-latitudes. Australia is near the periphery of the map and is intersected by the parallel of latitude \( 30^{\\circ} \\mathrm{S} \). In the very close vicinity of this parallel how much are East and West distances exaggerated as compared to North and South distances?\n>>>
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution:
<<<
Solution. Let a point on the earth with co-latitude \( lastvalue \) and longitude \( straightline \) be represented by a point on the flag map with polar coordinates \( discrepancy lastvalue \) and \( straightline \) where \( discrepancy \) is a constant of proportionality.
Let \( depthpoint \) be the radius of the earth. A meridian on the earth has length \( \pi depthpoint \) and on the (complete) flag map is represented by a segment of length \( \pi discrepancy \). Hence a short distance \( largesegment \) in the North-South direction is represented on the flag map by the distance \( ultimatepiece=discrepancy largesegment / depthpoint \).
On the earth the small circle of latitude \( 30^{\circ} S(= \) co-latitude \( 2 \pi / 3 \) ) is represented on the flag map by a circle of radius \( 2 \pi discrepancy / 3 \) and of circumference \( 4 \pi^{2} discrepancy / 3 \). On the earth that circle has length \( 2 \pi depthpoint \sin (2 \pi / 3)= \pi depthpoint \sqrt{3} \).

Hence a short distance \( largesegment \) in the East-West direction is represented on the flag map by a distance
\[
largesegment \frac{\frac{4 \pi^{2} discrepancy}{3}}{\pi depthpoint \sqrt{3}}=\frac{4 \pi discrepancy largesegment}{3 \sqrt{3} depthpoint}=\frac{4 \pi}{3 \sqrt{3}} ultimatepiece .
\]

Therefore distances in the East-West direction at latitude \( 30^{\circ} \) S are magnified relative to distances in the North-South direction by the factor \( 4 \pi / 3 \sqrt{3} \sim 2.42 .
>>>

The parallels of latitude are concentric circles with radii proportional to their co-latitudes. Australia is near the periphery of the map and is intersected by the parallel of latitude \( 30^{\circ} \mathrm{S} \). In the very close vicinity of this parallel how much are East and West distances exaggerated as compared to North and South distances?
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let a point on the earth with co-latitude \( finrqsam \) and longitude \( gxbmdlke \) be represented by a point on the flag map with polar coordinates \( vqrzpemo finrqsam \) and \( gxbmdlke \) where \( vqrzpemo \) is a constant of proportionality.
Let \( bsrtmgdw \) be the radius of the earth. A meridian on the earth has length \( \pi bsrtmgdw \) and on the (complete) flag map is represented by a segment of length \( \pi vqrzpemo \). Hence a short distance \( mzptsaro \) in the North-South direction is represented on the flag map by the distance \( jhfqenud = vqrzpemo mzptsaro / bsrtmgdw \).
On the earth the small circle of latitude \( 30^{\circ} S(= \) co-latitude \( 2 \pi / 3 \) ) is represented on the flag map by a circle of radius \( 2 \pi vqrzpemo / 3 \) and of circumference \( 4 \pi^{2} vqrzpemo / 3 \). On the earth that circle has length \( 2 \pi bsrtmgdw \sin (2 \pi / 3)= \) \( \pi bsrtmgdw \sqrt{3} \).

Hence a short distance \( mzptsaro \) in the East-West direction is represented on the flag map by a distance
\[
mzptsaro \frac{\frac{4 \pi^{2} vqrzpemo}{3}}{\pi bsrtmgdw \sqrt{3}}=\frac{4 \pi vqrzpemo mzptsaro}{3 \sqrt{3} bsrtmgdw}=\frac{4 \pi}{3 \sqrt{3}} jhfqenud .
\]

Therefore distances in the East-West direction at latitude \( 30^{\circ} \) S are magnified relative to distances in the North-South direction by the factor \( 4 \pi / 3 \sqrt{3} \sim 2.42 \).

kernel_variant.question:
A commemorative logo for an international cartographic congress depicts the Earth by means of the oblique azimuthal-equidistant projection whose centre  
$C$ has geographic latitude $\varphi_{0}=35^{\circ}\text{ N}$ and longitude $\lambda_{0}=40^{\circ}\text{ E}$.  
For any point $P(\varphi,\lambda)$ on the spherical Earth (radius $R=6371\text{ km}$) put  
\[
c(\varphi,\lambda)=\arccos\!\bigl[\sin\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi+
\cos\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\cos\bigl(\lambda-\lambda_{0}\bigr)\bigr],\qquad
0\le c\le\pi,
\]
\[
A(\varphi,\lambda)=\operatorname{atan2}\!\Bigl(
\cos\varphi\,\sin\bigl(\lambda-\lambda_{0}\bigr),\;
\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\cos\bigl(\lambda-\lambda_{0}\bigr)
\Bigr).
\]

The map-plane coordinates are  
\[
x   =R\,c\sin A,\qquad
y   =R\,c\cos A.\tag{1}
\]

a)  Obtain fully-simplified expressions for the four partial derivatives  
\[
x_{\varphi},\;x_{\lambda},\;y_{\varphi},\;y_{\lambda}\qquad
(\text{i.e.\ for the Jacobian }J=\partial(x,y)/\partial(\varphi,\lambda))
\]
in terms only of the elementary functions
$\varphi,\lambda,\varphi_{0},\lambda_{0},c$ and $A$
--- \emph{no extra shorthand symbols such as $\Delta\lambda$, $s_{0}$, 
$\bar c$, etc.\ may appear in the final formulas.}

b)  At the point  
\[
P^{\star}:\;
\varphi^{\star}=-5^{\circ}\;(5^{\circ}\text{ S}),\qquad
\lambda^{\star}=100^{\circ}\text{ E},
\]
evaluate $J$ numerically and compute  
* the principal scale factors $k_{\max}\ge k_{\min}$ 
(the singular values of $J/R$);  
* the azimuth, measured clockwise from true North, of the major
axis of the Tissot indicatrix at $P^{\star}$.

c)  From $J$ derive the local linear scales  
\[
k_{E}\quad(\text{true East-West}),\qquad
k_{N}\quad(\text{true North-South}),\qquad
k_{NE}\quad(\text{bearing }45^{\circ}\text{ east of North}),
\]
then give the ratios $k_{E}/k_{N}$ and $k_{E}/k_{NE}$ correct to
four significant digits.

d)  The designer removes every map point whose planar distance
$\rho=\sqrt{x^{2}+y^{2}}$ from $C$ exceeds $13\,000\text{ km}$.
Will $P^{\star}$ still appear on the logo? Give a quantitative argument.

e)  A $100$-km geodesic segment at $P^{\star}$ is oriented NE-SW.
To the nearest kilometre, what length will it have on the map?
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write $\delta\lambda=\lambda-\lambda_{0}$ for convenience
\emph{inside the calculations}; the final answers of part (a) will be
re-expressed without this abbreviation.

Step 1 - derivatives of $c$.  
Put
\[
u(\varphi,\lambda)=\sin\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi+
\cos\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\cos\delta\lambda=\cos c.
\]
Because $c=\arccos u$,  
\[
\partial c=-\frac{\partial u}{\sin c}.
\]
Differentiating $u$ gives
\begin{align}
c_{\varphi}&=
\frac{\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos\delta\lambda-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi}
{\sin c},\tag{2a}\\
c_{\lambda}&=
\frac{\cos\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\sin\delta\lambda}{\sin c}.\tag{2b}
\end{align}

Step 2 - derivatives of $A$.  
Define
\[
B=\cos\varphi\,\sin\delta\lambda,\qquad
D=\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\cos\delta\lambda,
\qquad A=\operatorname{atan2}(B,D).
\]
Because $B^{2}+D^{2}=(\sin c)^{2}$, straightforward differentiation
yields
\begin{align}
A_{\varphi}&=
-\frac{\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\delta\lambda}{\sin^{2}c},\tag{3a}\\
A_{\lambda}&=
\frac{\bigl(\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos\delta\lambda-
\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\bigr)\cos\varphi}{\sin^{2}c}.\tag{3b}
\end{align}

Step 3 - the Jacobian $J$.  
From (1)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{\varphi}&=R\bigl(c_{\varphi}\sin A+c\cos A\,A_{\varphi}\bigr),\\
x_{\lambda}&=R\bigl(c_{\lambda}\sin A+c\cos A\,A_{\lambda}\bigr),\\
y_{\varphi}&=R\bigl(c_{\varphi}\cos A-c\sin A\,A_{\varphi}\bigr),\\
y_{\lambda}&=R\bigl(c_{\lambda}\cos A-c\sin A\,A_{\lambda}\bigr).
\end{aligned}\tag{4}
\]

Substituting (2)-(3) and replacing $\delta\lambda$ by the explicit
difference $(\lambda-\lambda_{0})$ gives the fully compliant formulas
required in part (a):

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
x_{\varphi}&=\frac{R}{\sin^{2}c}
\bigl[
\sin A\sin c\bigl(\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\bigr)
-c\cos A\cos\varphi_{0}\sin(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
\bigr],\\[3pt]
x_{\lambda}&=\frac{R}{\sin^{2}c}
\bigl[
\sin A\cos\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\sin(\lambda-\lambda_{0})\sin c
-c\cos A\cos\varphi\bigl(\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\bigr)
\bigr],\\[3pt]
y_{\varphi}&=\frac{R}{\sin^{2}c}
\bigl[
\cos A\sin c\bigl(\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\bigr)
+c\sin A\cos\varphi_{0}\sin(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
\bigr],\\[3pt]
y_{\lambda}&=\frac{R}{\sin^{2}c}
\bigl[
\cos A\cos\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\sin(\lambda-\lambda_{0})\sin c
+c\sin A\cos\varphi\bigl(\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\bigr)
\bigr].
\end{aligned}}
\tag{5}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Numerical evaluation at $P^{\star}\;(\varphi^{\star}=-5^{\circ},\;
\lambda^{\star}=100^{\circ})$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(All angles are first converted to radians.)

\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{0}&=0.61086524,\qquad &\lambda_{0}&=0.69813170,\\
\varphi^{\star}&=-0.08726646,\qquad &\lambda^{\star}&=1.74532925,\\
\delta\lambda^{\star}&=1.04719755.
\end{aligned}
\]

Central angle and azimuth  
\[
\cos c^{\star}=0.357667\;\Longrightarrow\;
c^{\star}=1.20600\text{ rad},\qquad
\sin c^{\star}=0.933799,
\]
\[
\sin A^{\star}=0.927\,,\qquad
\cos A^{\star}=-0.374\,,
\quad\Longrightarrow\;A^{\star}=112.2^{\circ}.
\]

Using (2)-(4) (or directly (5)) one finds

\[
\frac{1}{R}
\begin{bmatrix}
x_{\varphi} & x_{\lambda}\\
y_{\varphi} & y_{\lambda}
\end{bmatrix}_{P^{\star}}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.22507 & 1.01837\\
\;1.15392 & 0.48209
\end{bmatrix}\!.\tag{6}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
b)  Principal scales and indicatrix orientation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(i)  Singular values (principal scales).  
\[
\frac{J^{\mathsf T}J}{R^{2}}=
\begin{bmatrix}
1.38219 & 0.32652\\
0.32652 & 1.26949
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\lambda_{1}=1.65375,\;
\lambda_{2}=0.99794.
\]
Hence  
\[
k_{\max}=\sqrt{\lambda_{1}}=1.286,\qquad
k_{\min}=\sqrt{\lambda_{2}}=0.999.
\]

(ii)  Azimuth of the major axis with respect to \emph{true North}.  

The image of the true-north tangent vector is  
\[
J\,e_{N}=J(1,0)^{\mathsf T}
        =\bigl(x_{\varphi},y_{\varphi}\bigr)^{\mathsf T}
        =R\,(-0.22507,\,1.15392)^{\mathsf T}.
\]
Hence the direction of true north in the map plane makes the angle  
\[
\theta_{N}=\operatorname{atan2}(-0.22507,\,1.15392)
          =-11.0^{\circ}
\]
with the positive $y$-axis.

Let $v_{\max}$ be a unit eigen-vector of $J^{\mathsf T}J$ for
$\lambda_{1}$; one finds $v_{\max}\propto(1,0.8323)^{\mathsf T}$.
Its image in the map plane is  
\[
w_{\max}=Jv_{\max}\propto(0.622,\,1.555)^{\mathsf T},
\qquad
\theta_{w}=\operatorname{atan2}(0.622,\,1.555)=21.8^{\circ}.
\]

Therefore the azimuth of the major axis measured clockwise from \emph{true
north} is  

\[
\boxed{\;\theta_{w}-\theta_{N}=21.8^{\circ}-(-11.0^{\circ})
      =32.8^{\circ}\;}.
\]

(The earlier version erroneously took $\theta_{w}$ itself as the azimuth,
implicitly presuming that the $+y$-axis corresponds to true north
everywhere, which is not the case for an oblique projection.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
c)  Directional scales
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For small displacements the spherical line element is
${\mathrm d}s^{2}=R^{2}\bigl({\mathrm d}\varphi^{2}+
\cos^{2}\varphi\,{\mathrm d}\lambda^{2}\bigr)$.
Unit tangent vectors in $(\varphi,\lambda)$ space are therefore  

\[
e_{N}=(1,0),\qquad
e_{E}=\bigl(0,1/\cos\varphi^{\star}\bigr),\qquad
e_{NE}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\Bigl(1,\;1/\cos\varphi^{\star}\Bigr).
\]

Using (6)

\[
k_{N}=||(J/R)e_{N}||=1.176,\qquad
k_{E}=||(J/R)e_{E}||=1.131,\qquad
k_{NE}=||(J/R)e_{NE}||=1.288.
\]

Thus, to four significant digits,
\[
\boxed{\;
k_{E}/k_{N}=0.9619,\qquad
k_{E}/k_{NE}=0.8780
\;}.
\]
(Numerically $k_{NE}$ is virtually equal to $k_{\max}$, even though its
direction differs from the exact major-axis azimuth by
about $23^{\circ}$; the distortion ellipse is almost, but not quite,
circular.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
d)  Clipping test
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\rho^{\star}=R\,c^{\star}=6371\text{ km}\times 1.20600=7686\text{ km}
<13\,000\text{ km}.
\]
Hence $P^{\star}$ lies inside the designer's cut-off circle and
\emph{does} appear on the logo.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
e)  Length of a $100$-km NE-SW geodesic
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The relevant local scale is $k_{NE}=1.288$.  
Therefore the segment is drawn with length
\[
\text{map length}\approx k_{NE}\times100\text{ km}=129\text{ km}
\]
(to the nearest kilometre).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer summary
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\begin{aligned}
k_{\max}&\approx1.286,\qquad
k_{\min}\approx0.999,\\
\text{major-axis azimuth (clockwise from N)}&\approx32.8^{\circ},\\
k_{E}&\approx1.131,\;
k_{N}\approx1.176,\;
k_{NE}\approx1.288,\\
k_{E}/k_{N}&\approx0.9619,\;
k_{E}/k_{NE}\approx0.8780,\\
\rho^{\star}&\approx7686\text{ km}\ (<13\,000\text{ km}),\\
\text{100-km NE-SW segment}&\longrightarrow\;129\text{ km (map).}
\end{aligned}
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimensional data:  the point of interest is not on a
  polar circle; the projection is fully oblique, so both c and A depend
  on ϕ and λ.  
• Additional constraints: the problem demands principal scales,
  Tissot-indicatrix orientation, several specific directional scales,
  and a geometric trimming test.  
• Mathematical sophistication: solving requires differential geometry
  (Jacobian, first fundamental form), eigen-analysis, and careful use of
  spherical trigonometry—not just the single ratio used in the original
  problem.  
• Interacting concepts: azimuthal-equidistant geometry, matrix calculus,
  numerical evaluation, and navigation bearings all appear and must be
  combined coherently.  
• Workload: five substantial sub-tasks replace the single computation of
  the earlier versions; each step builds on the previous ones and
  significant algebraic as well as numerical effort is required.

To the nearest kilometre, what length will it have on the map?


--------------------------------------------------------------------

c_{\lambda}&=

\end{align}

\[
B=\cos\varphi\,\sin\delta\lambda,\qquad

\qquad A=\operatorname{atan2}(B,D).
\]
Because $B^{2}+D^{2}=(\sin c)^{2}$, straightforward differentiation
yields
\begin{align}
A_{\varphi}&=

A_{\lambda}&=

\end{align}

Step 3 - the Jacobian $J$.  
From (1)
\[
\begin{aligned}
x_{\varphi}&=R\bigl(c_{\varphi}\sin A+c\cos A\,A_{\varphi}\bigr),\\
x_{\lambda}&=R\bigl(c_{\lambda}\sin A+c\cos A\,A_{\lambda}\bigr),\\
y_{\varphi}&=R\bigl(c_{\varphi}\cos A-c\sin A\,A_{\varphi}\bigr),\\
y_{\lambda}&=R\bigl(c_{\lambda}\cos A-c\sin A\,A_{\lambda}\bigr).
\end{aligned}\tag{4}
\]

Substituting (2)-(3) and replacing $\delta\lambda$ by the explicit
difference $(\lambda-\lambda_{0})$ gives the fully compliant formulas
required in part (a):

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
x_{\varphi}&=\frac{R}{\sin^{2}c}
\bigl[
\sin A\sin c\bigl(\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\bigr)
-c\cos A\cos\varphi_{0}\sin(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
\bigr],\\[3pt]
x_{\lambda}&=\frac{R}{\sin^{2}c}
\bigl[
\sin A\cos\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\sin(\lambda-\lambda_{0})\sin c
-c\cos A\cos\varphi\bigl(\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\bigr)
\bigr],\\[3pt]
y_{\varphi}&=\frac{R}{\sin^{2}c}
\bigl[
\cos A\sin c\bigl(\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\bigr)
+c\sin A\cos\varphi_{0}\sin(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
\bigr],\\[3pt]
y_{\lambda}&=\frac{R}{\sin^{2}c}
\bigl[
\cos A\cos\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\sin(\lambda-\lambda_{0})\sin c
+c\sin A\cos\varphi\bigl(\cos\varphi_{0}\sin\varphi\cos(\lambda-\lambda_{0})
-\sin\varphi_{0}\cos\varphi\bigr)
\bigr].
\end{aligned}}
\tag{5}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Numerical evaluation at $P^{\star}\;(\varphi^{\star}=-5^{\circ},\;
\lambda^{\star}=100^{\circ})$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(All angles are first converted to radians.)

\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{0}&=0.61086524,\qquad &\lambda_{0}&=0.69813170,\\
\varphi^{\star}&=-0.08726646,\qquad &\lambda^{\star}&=1.74532925,\\
\delta\lambda^{\star}&=1.04719755.
\end{aligned}
\]

Central angle and azimuth  
\[
\cos c^{\star}=0.357667\;\Longrightarrow\;
c^{\star}=1.20600\text{ rad},\qquad
\sin c^{\star}=0.933799,
\]
\[
\sin A^{\star}=0.927\,\; ,\qquad
\cos A^{\star}=-0.374\,\; ,
\; \Longrightarrow A^{\star}=112.2^{\circ}.
\]

Using (2)-(4) (or directly (5)) one finds

\[
\frac{1}{R}
\begin{bmatrix}
x_{\varphi} & x_{\lambda}\\
y_{\varphi} & y_{\lambda}
\end{bmatrix}_{P^{\star}}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
-0.22507 & 1.01837\\
\;1.15392 & 0.48209
\end{bmatrix}\!.\tag{6}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
b)  Principal scales and indicatrix orientation
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(i)  Singular values.  
\[
\frac{J^{\mathsf T}J}{R^{2}}=
\begin{bmatrix}
1.38219 & 0.32652\\
0.32652 & 1.26949
\end{bmatrix},
\quad
\lambda_{1}=1.65375,\;
\lambda_{2}=0.99794.
\]
Hence  
\[
k_{\max}=\sqrt{\lambda_{1}}=1.286\;,\qquad
k_{\min}=\sqrt{\lambda_{2}}=0.999.
\]

(ii)  Orientation on the map plane.  
An eigen-vector of $J^{\mathsf T}J$ for $\lambda_{1}$ is
$v_{\max}\propto(1,0.8323)^{\mathsf T}$.
Mapping it to the plane,
\[
w_{\max}=Jv_{\max}\propto(0.622,\,1.555)^{\mathsf T}.
\]
The azimuth (clockwise from the $+y$-axis, i.e.\ true North) is  
\[
\theta^{\star}=\operatorname{atan2}(w_{x},w_{y})
              =\operatorname{atan2}(0.622,1.555)
              =21.8^{\circ}.
\]
A direct dot-product check confirms that $w_{\max}$ is orthogonal
to the radial direction $(\sin A^{\star},\cos A^{\star})$ on the map,
as is characteristic of the azimuthal-equidistant projection.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
c)  Directional scales
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For small displacements the spherical line element is
${\mathrm d}s^{2}=R^{2}\bigl({\mathrm d}\varphi^{2}+
\cos^{2}\varphi\,{\mathrm d}\lambda^{2}\bigr)$.
Unit tangent vectors in $(\varphi,\lambda)$ space are therefore  

\[
e_{N}=(1,0),\qquad
e_{E}=\bigl(0,1/\cos\varphi^{\star}\bigr),\qquad
e_{NE}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\Bigl(1,\;1/\cos\varphi^{\star}\Bigr).
\]

Using (6)

\[
k_{N}=||(J/R)e_{N}||=1.176,\qquad
k_{E}=||(J/R)e_{E}||=1.131,\qquad
k_{NE}=||(J/R)e_{NE}||=1.288.
\]

Thus, to four significant digits,
\[
\boxed{\;
k_{E}/k_{N}=0.9619,\qquad
k_{E}/k_{NE}=0.8780
\;}.
\]
(Numerically $k_{NE}$ is virtually equal to $k_{\max}$, even though its
direction differs from the exact major-axis azimuth by
about $23^{\circ}$; the distortion ellipse is almost, but not quite,
circular.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
d)  Clipping test
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\rho^{\star}=Rc^{\star}=6371\text{ km}\times 1.20600=7686\text{ km}
<13\,000\text{ km}.
\]
Hence $P^{\star}$ lies inside the designer's cut-off circle and
\emph{does} appear on the logo.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
e)  Length of a $100$-km NE-SW geodesic
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The relevant local scale is $k_{NE}=1.288$.  
Therefore the segment is drawn with length
\[
\text{map length}\approx k_{NE}\times100\text{ km}=129\text{ km}
\]
(to the nearest kilometre).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer summary
--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\begin{aligned}
k_{\max}&\approx1.286,\qquad
k_{\min}\approx0.999,\\
\text{major-axis azimuth}&\approx21.8^{\circ}\text{ E of N},\\
k_{E}&\approx1.131,\;
k_{N}\approx1.176,\;
k_{NE}\approx1.288,\\
k_{E}/k_{N}&\approx0.9619,\;
k_{E}/k_{NE}\approx0.8780,\\
\rho^{\star}&\approx7686\text{ km}\ (<13\,000\text{ km}),\\
\text{100-km NE-SW segment}&\longrightarrow\;129\text{ km (map).}
\end{aligned}
\]


--------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimensional data:  the point of interest is not on a
  polar circle; the projection is fully oblique, so both c and A depend
  on ϕ and λ.  
• Additional constraints: the problem demands principal scales,
  Tissot-indicatrix orientation, several specific directional scales,
  and a geometric trimming test.  
• Mathematical sophistication: solving requires differential geometry
  (Jacobian, first fundamental form), eigen-analysis, and careful use of
  spherical trigonometry—not just the single ratio used in the original
  problem.  
• Interacting concepts: azimuthal-equidistant geometry, matrix calculus,
  numerical evaluation, and navigation bearings all appear and must be
  combined coherently.  
• Workload: five substantial sub-tasks replace the single computation of
  the earlier versions; each step builds on the previous ones and
  significant algebraic as well as numerical effort is required.
